content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here’s something that trips up almost everyone new to Bitcoin at the protocol level:
You don’t have a Bitcoin balance.
There is no account, no ledger row that says “jay: 0.5 BTC.” The number your wallet shows you is a calculation, not a stored value. Once that clicks, transactions suddenly make sense - and a lot of confusing behavior (change outputs, “stuck” transactions, weirdly high fees) becomes obvious.
Let’s build the real mental model from the ground up, with commands you can run against a Bitcoin node yourself.
Coins, not accounts: the UTXO model
Bitcoin doesn’t track balances. It tracks coins - specifically, UTXOs (Unspent Transaction Outputs). A UTXO is a discrete chunk of Bitcoin that was sent to you and hasn’t been spent yet. Think of them like physical bills and coins in a wallet: you might be holding a $20, a $5, and three $1s. You don’t have “a $28 balance” - you have five separate pieces of cash that add up to $28.
Your Bitcoin “balance” is exactly that: your wallet scans the blockchain, finds every UTXO locked to keys it controls, and sums them. Spend one, and it’s gone - replaced by new UTXOs created for whoever you paid.
This single design choice is why the rest of the system looks the way it does.
Anatomy of a transaction
Every Bitcoin transaction is just a mapping: it consumes some existing UTXOs (inputs) and creates new ones (outputs).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Two things to notice, because they’re the crux of everything:

• Inputs are references, not amounts. An input doesn’t say “0.4 BTC.” It points at a previous output - by transaction ID and output index (txid:vout) - and proves you're allowed to spend it.

• Inputs are spent whole. You can’t spend “part” of a UTXO. If you have a 0.4 BTC coin and want to send 0.1, you spend the entire 0.4 and send the remainder back to yourself. That remainder is the change output - the Bitcoin equivalent of getting change back when you pay for a $6 coffee with a $20.
Inputs
Each input has:

• The reference: txid (the transaction that created the coin) + vout (which output of that transaction).

• The unlocking data: a digital signature (and public key) proving you control the key the coin was locked to. In modern SegWit/Taproot transactions, this lives in the witness; in legacy transactions, it’s the scriptSig.
Outputs
Each output has:

• An amount (in satoshis - 1 BTC = 100,000,000 sats).

• A locking script (scriptPubKey) - the conditions that must be met to spend it later. For a normal payment, that condition is "provide a valid signature for this address."
The address you paste into a wallet is just a human-friendly encoding of that locking script. The prefix tells you the type:

• 1… - P2PKH: legacy pay-to-public-key-hash

• 3… - P2SH: pay-to-script-hash (e.g. multisig)

• bc1q… - P2WPKH: native SegWit

• bc1p… - P2TR: Taproot
Where do fees come from? (There is no fee field)
This surprises people: a Bitcoin transaction has no “fee” field. The fee is implicit - it’s simply whatever’s left over:
fee = (sum of all inputs) − (sum of all outputs)In the diagram above: 0.7 in − 0.6997 out = 0.0003 BTC is the fee. Nobody writes it down; miners just claim the difference. This is also why forgetting a change output is catastrophic - if you spend a 1 BTC input and only create a 0.01 BTC output, you just paid a 0.99 BTC fee. (Wallets add change automatically for exactly this reason.)
Fees aren’t priced in “BTC per transaction.” They’re priced by size, in satoshis per virtual byte (sat/vB). A transaction with many inputs is physically larger, so at the same fee rate, it costs more. When the mempool is congested, you outbid others by raising sat/vB - miners pick the most profitable transactions per unit of block space first.
Engineer’s note: this is why consolidating many small UTXOs when fees are low is good hygiene - a wallet full of “dust” (tiny UTXOs) becomes expensive to spend later, because each one adds bytes.
Locking and unlocking: a tiny bit of Script
Bitcoin outputs are guarded by Script, a deliberately limited, stack-based language. You don’t write it by hand, but knowing what it does demystifies “how does the coin know it’s mine?”

• The locking script (on the output) says, roughly: “To spend me, provide a public key that hashes to X, and a signature valid for it.”

• The unlocking script / witness (on the input, when you spend) provides exactly that signature and public key.
At validation time, a node runs them together; if the result is valid, the spend is allowed. Legacy spends use ECDSA signatures; Taproot (bc1p…) uses Schnorr, which is smaller and enables cleaner multisig. That's the whole "ownership" mechanism - no accounts, just locks and keys.
See it yourself with bitcoin-cli
If you run a node (bitcoind), you can dissect any transaction. Fetch one and decode it:
# Pull a transaction and decode it into readable JSON
bitcoin-cli getrawtransaction <txid> trueYou’ll get something shaped like this (trimmed):
{
"txid": "…",
"vsize": 141,
"vin": [
{ "txid": "abc…", "vout": 0, "txinwitness": ["<sig>", "<pubkey>"] }
],
"vout": [
{ "value": 0.60000000, "n": 0,
"scriptPubKey": { "address": "bc1q…recipient", "type": "witness_v0_keyhash" } },
{ "value": 0.09970000, "n": 1,
"scriptPubKey": { "address": "bc1q…yourchange", "type": "witness_v0_keyhash" } }
]
}Read it like the diagram: vin are the coins being spent (each pointing at a prior txid:vout), vout are the new coins. Sum the input values (look them up), subtract the output values, and you've computed the fee by hand. The vsize (141 vbytes here) times your fee rate is what you paid.
You can even build one manually to feel the model:
# 1. Construct: spend one input, create two outputs (recipient + change)
bitcoin-cli createrawtransaction \
'[{"txid":"abc…","vout":0}]' \
'{"bc1q…recipient":0.6, "bc1q…yourchange":0.0997}'# 2. Sign it with your wallet's keys
bitcoin-cli signrawtransactionwithwallet <hexfromstep1># 3. Broadcast to the network
bitcoin-cli sendrawtransaction <signedhex>Notice step 1 forces you to specify the change output yourself — the raw API won’t calculate it for you. Get the arithmetic wrong and the leftover becomes fee. That’s the UTXO model with the training wheels off.
The lifecycle: from your wallet to a block

• Build - your wallet selects UTXOs (coin selection), adds a change output, and estimates a fee rate.

• Sign - it produces signatures unlocking each input.

• Broadcast - the signed transaction propagates across the peer-to-peer network.

• Mempool - nodes hold it in their memory pool, unconfirmed, ranked by fee rate.

• Mined - a miner includes it in a block (roughly every 10 minutes).

• Confirmed - each block on top adds a confirmation. More confirmations = harder to reverse; ~6 is the classic “settled” bar for large amounts.
If fees spike after you broadcast, a low-fee transaction can sit in the mempool for hours — which is the entire reason fee bumping (RBF and CPFP) exists. (Good topic for a follow-up.)
The mental model, one more time

• Bitcoin has no balances - only UTXOs (coins) locked to keys.

• A transaction spends whole UTXOs and creates new ones; leftover goes back as change.

• The fee is implicit - inputs minus outputs - and priced by size (sat/vB), not by amount.

• Script locks each output; a signature unlocks it. No accounts, just locks and keys.
Once you stop thinking “balance” and start thinking “a wallet full of coins,” everything else — change, fees, dust, stuck transactions - stops being mysterious and starts being obvious.

Running your own node is the best way to internalize this - every command above works against bitcoind. If you'd like, I'll follow up with a hands-on piece on fee bumping (RBF & CPFP) for when a transaction gets stuck.
